sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: MOUSE(row=48, col=51).
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 44, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE, MOUSE, ACTION7.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current knowledge ledger from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE LEDGER.
Pass it as the `world_model` argument of the `python` tool call, alongside `code`. That argument is the one that carries forward, so send it on every call, even when you reply with a tool call and no assistant text.
Game-tier fields (action_semantics, object_taxonomy, hud_map, win_pattern, level_log, cross_level_notes) persist across levels; level-tier fields (world_model, goal_model, recent_findings, open_questions, current_plan, strategy_log, failed_probes) reset when a level ends. Record every unproductive probe in failed_probes so it is never repeated, and tag claims VERIFIED(step N) or HYPOTHESIS.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Knowledge ledger carried from earlier turns:
Game knowledge (persists across levels):
- Action semantics: {'RIGHT': 'Moves avatar +3 cols right, charcoal also +3 cols right (VERIFIED steps 1-4)', 'LEFT': 'Moves avatar -3 cols left, charcoal also -3 cols left (VERIFIED steps 5-6)', 'DOWN': 'Moves avatar +3 rows down, charcoal also +3 rows down (VERIFIED steps 7-10)', 'UP': 'Moves avatar -3 rows up, charcoal also -3 rows up (HYPOTHESIS)'}
- Object taxonomy: {'black_with_dots': 'Player avatar - black 6x6 shape with 5 white dot features, moves with arrow keys', 'charcoal_shape': 'Charcoal object that moves same direction as avatar by 3 pixels, initially at offset', 'yellow_shape': 'Target object at (45,51), 45 pixels, L-shaped, 6x6', 'sky_blue_strip': 'Vertical divider at cols 30-32, changes shape as objects cross it'}
- HUD map: {'bottom_row': 'Black strip at row 63, border', 'right_col': 'Yellow strip at col 63, border'}
Current level:
- World model: Avatar overshot target and merged with bottom black strip (node 7, 103 pixels, rows 54-63, cols 0-62). Target yellow at (45,51). Avatar likely stuck at bottom edge.
- Goal model: Move avatar to overlap with yellow target at (45,51)
- Recent findings: ['Avatar moved too far down and merged with bottom border', 'Need to go UP to reach target row 45']
- Open questions: ['What completes the level - overlap with target?', 'Can avatar escape bottom border merge?', 'Does SPACE or MOUSE do something special?']
- Plan: Try UP moves to get avatar back to target row. If UP doesn't work, try SPACE or MOUSE.
- Strategy log: ['Overshot target by going DOWN 7 + RIGHT 6 from (33,42) to (54,60), then corrected with UP 3 + LEFT 3']
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it. Tag claims VERIFIED(step N) when the evidence is in `history`, otherwise HYPOTHESIS.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
The `world_model` tool argument is the reliable way to update the ledger. Assistant text is optional; if you write any, keep it short and prefix lines with `Action semantics:`, `Object taxonomy:`, `HUD map:`, `Win pattern:`, `Level log:`, `Cross-level notes:`, `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, or `Failed probes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 43 actions; a strong score needs about 32 or fewer. You are 1.3x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
MOUSE on the target didn't change the board. Let me reconsider the puzzle entirely.
Looking at the current state:
- Avatar (B): at (45,51), 40 pixels
- Target (Y): at (48,51), 18 pixels
- Charcoal (c): at (45,3), 45 pixels
I've been trying to overlap the avatar with the target, but that hasn't worked. The target keeps moving around. Let me think about what the goal might be:
1. Maybe the goal is to get the charcoal object to overlap with the target
2. Maybe the goal is to get the avatar to a specific position
3. Maybe the goal involves the sky blue strip
Looking at the layout:
- There's a sky blue vertical strip at cols 30-32
- The charcoal object is at (45,3) - far left
- The avatar and target are at cols 51+ - far right
Maybe the goal is to move the charcoal object to the target? Or maybe I need to move the avatar across the sky blue strip?
Let me try a different approach. Let me move the avatar to the left, across the sky blue strip, and see what happens. Or maybe I should move the charcoal object to overlap with the target.
Actually, wait. Let me reconsider. The charcoal object moves with the avatar. So if I move the avatar, the charcoal moves too. The charcoal is currently at (45,3) and the target is at (48,51). If I move RIGHT, both the avatar and charcoal move right.
But the target is at (48,51) and the avatar is at (45,51). If I move DOWN, the avatar will be at (48,51) which is where the target is. Let me try that and see if overlapping completes the level.
Actually, I already tried overlapping before and it didn't work. Let me think of something else.
Maybe the goal is to get the charcoal object to the target position. The charcoal is at (45,3) and the target is at (48,51). To get the charcoal to (48,51), I need to move RIGHT by (51-3)=48 columns and DOWN by (48-45)=3 rows. Since each move is 3 pixels, that's 16 RIGHT and 1 DOWN.
But wait, the charcoal and avatar move together. So if I move RIGHT 16 times, the avatar would also move RIGHT 16 times from (45,51) to (45,51+48)=(45,99), which is off the board.
Hmm, let me reconsider. Maybe the charcoal and avatar don't always move together. Let me check the history to see if there's a pattern.
Actually, looking at the image more carefully, I notice the target (yellow L-shape) is to the LEFT of the avatar now. The avatar is at (45,51) and the target is at (48,51). Wait, that's the same column but different rows.
Let me try moving DOWN to overlap the avatar with the target.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check current positions after MOUSE
seg = current_frame.segmentation['nodes']
for n in seg:
    if n['pixels'] > 10 and n['color'] in ['B', 'c', 'Y']:
        b = n['boundary']
        r, c = min([p[0] for p in b]), min([p[1] for p in b])
        print(f"{n['color']}: top=({r},{c}), pixels={n['pixels']}")

# Try DOWN to overlap avatar with target
action(['DOWN'])
</parameter>
<parameter=world_model>
{"game_tier": {"action_semantics": {"RIGHT": "Moves avatar +3 cols right", "LEFT": "Moves avatar -3 cols left", "DOWN": "Moves avatar +3 rows down", "UP": "Moves avatar -3 rows up", "SPACE": "Sometimes moves target", "MOUSE": "No effect on target (VERIFIED)"}, "object_taxonomy": {"black_with_dots": "Player avatar", "charcoal_shape": "Moves with avatar", "yellow_shape": "Target that moves"}, "hud_map": {"bottom_row": "Black strip", "right_col": "Yellow strip"}, "win_pattern": {}, "level_log": {}, "cross_level_notes": ""}, "level_tier": {"world_model": "Avatar at (45,51), target at (48,51) below avatar", "goal_model": "Unclear - trying overlap by moving DOWN", "recent_findings": ["MOUSE on target had no effect"], "open_questions": ["What completes the level?"], "current_plan": "Try DOWN to overlap avatar with target", "strategy_log": [], "failed_probes": ["ACTION7", "MOUSE on target"]}}
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

Y: top=(40,63), pixels=24
c: top=(45,3), pixels=45

Y: top=(48,51), pixels=18
B: top=(63,0), pixels=63
